title: ELV-P1-PROB-001
description:
[NULL] destroyed the auth daemon's recursive checksum. Without a base case, every badge scan crashes the stack. Restore it before the first fragment is lost.

Implement hashPassword(String s) that recursively sums the ASCII value of every character in s. An empty string must return 0.

Input: a single string
Output: the sum of ASCII values of all characters

title: ELV-P1-PROB-002
description:
The badge scanner counts security characters in access codes recursively. [NULL] gutted the logic. Restore it before the auth layer rejects every card.

Implement countChar(String s, char target) that recursively counts how many times target appears in s. Return 0 if s is empty or target is not found.

Input: first line is a string, second line is a single character
Output: the number of times the character appears in the string

title: ELV-P1-PROB-003
description:
[NULL] reversed every exit label in the CCTV registry to cover their tracks. This recursive function reverses them back. It is broken. Fix it.

Implement reverseCode(String s) that recursively reverses the given string. An empty string should return an empty string.

Input: a single string
Output: the string with all characters in reverse order

title: ELV-P1-PROB-004
description:
Floor access codes are validated by a recursive digit checksum. The module is down. Restore it before the elevator rejects every request.

Implement sumDigits(int n) that recursively sums each digit of n. For example, sumDigits(4821) returns 4+8+2+1 = 15. Handle n = 0 by returning 0.

Input: a non-negative integer
Output: the sum of its digits

title: ELV-P2-PROB-001
description:
The corridor heat sensor grid feeds into this machine. [NULL] broke the scan function so it never flags anomalies. Restore it — the security relay needs a clean threat report.

Implement findHotCells(int[][] grid) that scans every cell and returns a comma-separated list of "[row,col]=value" for every cell with a value strictly greater than 80. Return "none" if no cell exceeds the threshold.

Input: first line contains rows and cols, followed by grid values row by row
Output: flagged cells as "[row,col]=value" joined by ", ", or "none"

title: ELV-P2-PROB-002
description:
[NULL] is hiding in wireless dead zones to avoid detection. The signal map is a grid — find every cell with signal strength strictly below 10. Expose the dead zones and the relay comes back online.

Implement findDeadZones(int[][] signal) that returns a comma-separated list of "[row,col]=value" for every cell with signal strength strictly less than 10. Return "none" if coverage is full.

Input: first line contains rows and cols, followed by signal strength values row by row
Output: dead zone cells as "[row,col]=value" joined by ", ", or "none"

title: ELV-P2-PROB-003
description:
Security needs a headcount to confirm the building is clear. The attendance grid uses 1 for present and 0 for absent. Count the occupied cells and report the total.

Implement countPresent(int[][] attendance) that counts every cell equal to 1 and returns the total count.

Input: first line contains rows and cols, followed by attendance values (0 or 1) row by row
Output: total number of cells with value 1

title: ELV-P2-PROB-004
description:
One server rack is running [NULL]'s process and overheating the room. Find the single hottest cell in the thermal grid. Return its location and temperature so we know where to cut power.

Implement findHotspot(int[][] temps) that finds the cell with the maximum value and returns it as "[row,col]=value". If multiple cells share the maximum, return the first one found (scanning top to bottom, left to right).

Input: first line contains rows and cols, followed by temperature values row by row
Output: hottest cell in format "[row,col]=value"

title: ELV-P3-PROB-001
description:
[NULL] wiped both card subclasses from the clearance system. Every card now returns UNKNOWN and every door stays locked. The base class AccessCard survived — restore both card types from scratch.

Write two inner static classes inside ClearanceManager:
- StudentCard extends AccessCard. Its constructor takes only a name. Access level is always 1. Its identify() returns the parent result with " [STUDENT]" appended.
- StaffCard extends AccessCard. Its constructor takes only a name. Access level is always 5. Its identify() returns the parent result with " [STAFF]" appended.

title: ELV-P3-PROB-002
description:
The alert system cannot classify severity anymore — [NULL] deleted the subclasses. Minor and critical alerts both come back blank. Restore both types.

Write two inner static classes inside ClearanceManager:
- MinorAlert extends SystemAlert. Its constructor takes only a message. Severity is always 1. Its describe() returns the parent result with " [MINOR]" appended.
- CriticalAlert extends SystemAlert. Its constructor takes only a message. Severity is always 9. Its describe() returns the parent result with " [CRITICAL]" appended.

title: ELV-P3-PROB-003
description:
The network device registry lost both its concrete types. Wired and wireless devices both report as unknown hardware. Restore them so the building network can come back online.

Write two inner static classes inside ClearanceManager:
- WiredDevice extends NetworkDevice. Its constructor takes only an id. Bandwidth is always 1000. Its report() returns the parent result with " [WIRED]" appended.
- WirelessDevice extends NetworkDevice. Its constructor takes only an id. Bandwidth is always 300. Its report() returns the parent result with " [WIRELESS]" appended.

title: ELV-P3-PROB-004
description:
Lab equipment tagging is broken — safe and hazardous items look identical. [NULL] deleted both subclasses. One wrong label and the evacuation fails. Restore them.

Write two inner static classes inside ClearanceManager:
- SafeEquipment extends LabItem. Its constructor takes only a code. Risk level is always 1. Its label() returns the parent result with " [SAFE]" appended.
- HazardEquipment extends LabItem. Its constructor takes only a code. Risk level is always 5. Its label() returns the parent result with " [HAZARD]" appended.

title: ELV-P6-PROB-001
description:
The elevator queue manager is down — [NULL] wiped the sort logic. The lift now skips floors at random. You are one floor from the exit. Sort the queue manually and get it moving in the right order.

Implement sortFloors(int[] floors) that sorts the array in ascending order in place. You may NOT use Arrays.sort() or any library sort method — write the sorting algorithm yourself.

Input: first line is n (number of floors), second line is n space-separated floor numbers
Output: sorted array in Arrays.toString() format, e.g. [1, 2, 3, 4, 5]

title: ELV-P6-PROB-002
description:
Critical floors need service first — the highest priority number wins. The priority sorter is gone and the lift is serving the wrong floors first. Restore the sort in descending order.

Implement sortByPriority(int[] priorities) that sorts the array in descending order in place. You may NOT use Arrays.sort() or any library sort method — write the sorting algorithm yourself.

Input: first line is n (number of priorities), second line is n space-separated priority values
Output: sorted array in descending order in Arrays.toString() format

title: ELV-P6-PROB-003
description:
Lab booking slots include duplicates — multiple people queued for the same floor. The sorter has to handle that cleanly. [NULL] knew it wouldn't. Fix it and get the queue sorted correctly.

Implement sortSlots(int[] slots) that sorts the array in ascending order in place, correctly preserving duplicate values. You may NOT use Arrays.sort() or any library sort method.

Input: first line is n (number of slots), second line is n space-separated slot numbers
Output: sorted array in Arrays.toString() format with duplicates preserved

title: ELV-P6-PROB-004
description:
The emergency system needs the three highest-risk floor scores to prioritise response. Sort the scores yourself, then return only the top three — no shortcuts, write the algorithm from scratch.

Implement topThreeScores(int[] scores) that sorts all scores in descending order and returns a new int[] containing only the top 3 values. You may NOT use Arrays.sort() or any library sort method. Input will always have at least 3 elements.

Input: first line is n (number of scores), second line is n space-separated scores
Output: top 3 scores in descending order in Arrays.toString() format, e.g. [91, 88, 72]
